title: Grafting queen cells
subtitle: Taking larvae from the brood frame and placing them in prepared cups for the colony to raise as queens.
excerpt: Grafting is the beekeeper's primary method for raising queens from selected stock. A young larva is transferred to a wax or plastic queen cup, placed in a queenless or cell-raising colony, and the bees build a full queen cell around it. The work takes about an hour and requires good lighting, the right larval age, and a colony prepared to accept the grafts.
sourceNotes: BBKA examination syllabus, Module 4: Bee Breeding. National Bee Unit BeeBase: Practical Queen Rearing guide. Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Internet Archive, chapter on queen-rearing.

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of transferring a very young larva (ideally 12-24 hours old) from a worker cell into a prepared queen cup, to be raised as a queen by the colony. The central operation in most queen-rearing systems.

A small wax or plastic cell shaped like the bottom of a queen cell, attached to a wooden bar and used as the starting point for a grafted larva. The bees draw the cup out into a full queen cell as the larva develops.
A colony set up to accept and build grafted queen cells. Usually a strong colony made queenless for 24 hours before the graft is introduced, so the bees are motivated to raise queens.

A protein-rich secretion produced by nurse bees from hypopharyngeal glands, fed to all young larvae for the first three days and exclusively to queen larvae throughout development. The quantity and composition of royal jelly determines whether a larva develops into a queen or a worker.

content.text: Grafting
is the skilled centre of most queen-rearing systems. A larva no more than 24 hours old is lifted from the worker comb and placed in a prepared
queen cup
. If the graft is accepted, the
cell raiser
floods it with
royal jelly
and draws out a full cell. The technique requires good lighting, a steady hand, and preparation done the day before.
When to graft

The optimum window is late April through June, when colonies are at full strength, nurse bees are plentiful, and the colony is in the mood to raise queens. Drone brood should be present and capped on the parent colony, which indicates conditions are right for mating. Avoid grafting during a dearth when there is no incoming nectar and the colony is defensive.
Setting up the cell raiser
Twenty-four hours before grafting, remove the queen from your strongest colony into a
nucleus hive

with three frames of brood and a frame of stores. The remaining colony, now queenless, will be highly motivated to raise queens when you return with the grafts. Check 24 hours later that the bees have not started an emergency queen cell on an existing larva; if they have, remove it.
Choosing the larvae
You need larvae from the best colony in your apiary: the one with the quietest temperament, best brood pattern, and lowest varroa load.
Read the brood frame

carefully to find larvae of the correct age. You need larvae that are no larger than 1.5 mm (newly hatched, still lying curled in the base of the cell, just visible to the naked eye). Larger larvae are too old and produce inferior queens. A headlamp or a bright white LED torch pointing across the comb helps enormously.
The graft

Prepare your queen cups (wax or plastic cups are both effective) on a bar that fits in the brood chamber. Moisten each cup with a drop of royal jelly or cooled boiled water; this helps the larva float into place and reduces desiccation during transfer.

The grafting tool is a fine curved probe or a Chinese grafting tool (a flexible loop of plastic that slides under the larva). Slide the tool under the larva at the shallow end of the cell, lifting it gently on a small pool of royal jelly. Lower it into the queen cup without rolling or flipping the larva. The larva should lie in the same orientation it had in the worker cell: floating on royal jelly, not dragged or tilted.

Work quickly. Larvae exposed to open air for more than two to three minutes are less likely to be accepted. Do ten to fifteen grafts per session; acceptance rates of 60-80% are normal for an experienced grafter.
Placing the bar

Take the grafted bar into the cell raiser within ten minutes of completing the grafts. Place it between the two frames of youngest open brood, where nurse bee density is highest. Close the hive and leave for 24 hours undisturbed.
Checking acceptance

After 24 hours, open the cell raiser briefly and check whether the bees have started drawing out cells around the grafts. Accepted cups will show new wax built up around the rim, with royal jelly visible inside. Rejected cups look untouched. Note how many were accepted and close up without disturbing the developing cells.
After sealing

Queen cells are sealed on day eight after the egg was laid (day six after hatching). Sealed cells can be moved to prepared nucleus hives on day nine or ten, but must be handled carefully and never shaken or inverted. See
introducing a mated queen
for what to do once the queen has mated and is ready to introduce to a colony.
What can go wrong
No cells accepted
Larvae were too old, queen was not fully removed, or the cell raiser had a virgin queen the beekeeper missed.
Check the cell raiser for queen cells or a mated queen before the next attempt. Try with younger larvae from a freshly-hatched comb.
Cells started but not built out
Cell raiser lost confidence; often due to an emergency queen cell elsewhere in the box taking priority, or a break in the dearth cycle.
Search the cell raiser for all brood frames, remove any emergency cells, and re-graft. Feed 1:1 sugar syrup to simulate a nectar flow.
